title: Patterns of Withdrawal: Adorno and the Beats
author: Giannakakis

opening_move.description: Both Adorno's negative dialectics and the Beat Generation emerged as radical responses to post-WWII social and ideological landscapes, united by scepticism toward instrumental rationality and shared dedication to non-identity, dissonance, and withdrawal. The puzzle: do these apparently disparate traditions share a structural logic of critical disaffiliation?
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Adorno's theoretical critique of late-capitalist reality and the Beats' literary/performative rejection of bourgeois norms converge in a shared endeavour of critical disaffiliation — negation and withdrawal as active forms of critique, not passive retreat.

rivals_engaged: standard readings of Beats as mere countercultural escapism
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper constructs a comparative framework between Adorno's theoretical writings (Negative Dialectics, Minima Moralia) and pivotal Beat interventions (Ginsberg's Howl, Kerouac's On the Road) to show structural and historical parallels: negation, non-identity, withdrawal from exchange relations, resistance to ideological closure.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close reading of Adorno's Negative Dialectics and Minima Moralia alongside Beat literary texts (Howl, On the Road, Doctor Sax); comparative cultural-philosophical analysis.
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Comparative philosophical and literary reading; structural homology analysis between theoretical and literary texts.
epistemic_mode.notes: The Beat literary texts function as interpretive cases alongside Adorno's theoretical writings. The case is generative: the parallel reading generates the concept of 'critical disaffiliation' as a shared pattern. This is Type 3a (canonical texts as philosophical mirror) — both Adorno and Beat texts are read as philosophical/cultural objects expressing a common grammar of negation.

case_type.case_name: Allen Ginsberg (Howl) and Jack Kerouac (On the Road, Doctor Sax) — Beat Generation literary corpus

normative_stake.domain: critical theory / culture / resistance / late capitalism

confidence_notes: Type 3a (canonical texts as philosophical mirror): both Adorno's theoretical texts and Beat literary texts are read as philosophical objects expressing a shared grammar of negation and withdrawal. The case function is generative: the comparative reading produces the concept of 'critical disaffiliation'. Differs from the TCS film-case papers (Type 3) in that both cases are textual/philosophical rather than cultural-popular media.
